serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter intended for 1 to 2 persons, estimated at approximately 300 g total, comprising assorted cheeses, cured meats, crackers, fruit, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese, brie; Cheese, cheddar; Salami, dry or hard; Crackers, standard; Grapes, raw — composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a mixed cheese and charcuterie platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of typical platter components: ~45% assorted cheeses (brie, cheddar, gouda), ~25% cured meats (salami, prosciutto), ~15% crackers, ~10% fresh fruit (grapes), ~5% nuts and condiments. Component values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. Weighted average applied per 100 g of combined platter weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a mixed cheese and charcuterie platter. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous mixed cheese and cracker combinations. Cheese has a GI of approximately 0–10 (negligible carbohydrate); crackers contribute the primary glycemic load (GI ~50–65 for plain crackers). Cured meats have negligible GI. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~40 (low category, ≤55) reflecting the dominant low-GI cheese and meat components diluting the cracker contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 48.0) / 100 = 19.2, where serving carbohydrate = 16.0 g/100g × 300 g serving = 48.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured meats and aged cheeses
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter due to its high sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g, approximately 1860 mg per full serving), which substantially contributes to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from crackers; high saturated fat from cheese
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this platter in small portions, as the low overall GI (~40) is favourable, but the cracker component raises glycemic load and the high saturated fat content (~11.5 g/100 g) may worsen insulin resistance; portion control and cracker substitution with low-GI alternatives are advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and sodium from cheese and cured meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this platter sparingly, as the combination of high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g), high sodium (~620 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (~65 mg/100 g) from aged cheeses and cured meats poses significant cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly or in large portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Cheese and Charcuterie Platter (marketed as 'Love & Cheese Platter for 1 to 2 Pax' at EC Coffee Bar, Carpenter Street, Singapore) is a Western-style café sharing board designed for one to two diners. It typically comprises an assortment of aged and soft cheeses such as brie, cheddar, and gouda, accompanied by cured meats including salami and prosciutto, plain or seeded crackers, fresh fruit such as grapes or figs, mixed nuts, and sweet condiments such as honey or fruit jam. The platter is served cold and assembled without cooking. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and complex, with contrasting textures ranging from creamy soft cheese to crisp crackers and chewy cured meats, balanced by the sweetness of fruit and honey. Nutritionally, the platter is calorie-dense (~320 kcal per 100 g) and high in fat, particularly saturated fat (~11.5 g/100 g) and protein (~14.5 g/100 g), with moderate carbohydrate (~16 g/100 g) primarily from crackers and fruit. Sodium content is notably high (~620 mg/100 g) due to the cured meats and aged cheeses, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low category), reflecting the dominant low-GI cheese and meat components. Calcium content is significant (~220 mg/100 g) from the cheese components. This dish is not suitable for vegans, is not halal-certified due to pork-based charcuterie, and contains common allergens including milk, gluten, and tree nuts.
common_user_queries: What is in a cheese platter?
Calories in cheese and charcuterie board
Is cheese platter suitable for diabetics?
EC Coffee Bar cheese platter nutrition

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific platter. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-average composite of typical platter components using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the platter composition is well-characterised for this café style.
notes: 1. This record corresponds to the 'Love & Cheese Platter (for 1 to 2 Pax)' sold at EC Coffee Bar, Carpenter Street, Singapore, sourced from field survey data (GD003542). The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Cheese and Charcuterie Platter' for database consistency. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-average composite of typical platter components (45% assorted cheeses, 25% cured meats, 15% crackers, 10% fresh fruit, 5% nuts and condiments) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. 3. GI is an analogous estimate of 40 (low, ≤55) based on the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) for comparable cheese and cracker combinations. 4. Serving size estimated at 300 g for a 1–2 person platter based on standard café platter conventions; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA component data and are not confirmed zero; all are reported as positive numeric estimates. 6. The platter is not halal-certified due to the presence of pork-based charcuterie (salami, prosciutto). 7. Allergens include milk (cheese), gluten (crackers), and tree nuts; individuals with these allergies should avoid this dish. 8. Cuisine is classified as Western/Café rather than Malaysian despite the field survey source tag, as the dish is a Western-style cheese and charcuterie board served at a Malaysian-owned café in Singapore.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list — all entries confirmed present verbatim. Verified GI category threshold (GI=40 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed correct). Verified all ontology match_type values are within permitted set. Verified no -1 sentinel values used (all fields populated with estimates). Verified zero-macro rule not triggered (all core macros > 0). Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass → PASS.
